2001-03-01.title: Rwanda and Uganda Begin Pulling Troops From Congo
2001-03-01.abstract: In major step toward peace in Congo, armies of Rwanda and Uganda pull back soldiers from front-line positions with expectation that other combatants will do same soon; Rwanda and Uganda, which are two of six outside nations fighting in Congo, make clear they are not withdrawing their forces entirely; but their latest moves are most hopeful sign since August 1999, when most combatants signed accord they all promptly ignored; foreign diplomats react cautiously to latest events, warning of many failures in past; photo; map (M)

2001-03-01.title: Bomb in Northern Israel Kills 1, Wounds 9
2001-03-01.abstract: JERUSALEM, March 1 — A taxi van exploded in northern Israel today after police stopped it at a roadblock during a search for a terror suspect. As officers were checking passengers' identification cards, a bomb aboard was detonated, killing one person and wounding nine others.

2001-03-01.title: Spy Case Ends for American Held on Russia Drug Charge
2001-03-01.abstract: Russian Federal Security Service says it has no further interest in John Edward Tobin, American student arrested on drug charges last month but accused of training to become American military spy; other Russian officials indicate they might charge him with more serious violations of narcotics law that could lead to lengthy prison sentence; Tobin is graduate student in Voronezh, under State Dept Fulbright scholar program; he was arrested Feb 1 outside nightclub after police found what they say was marijuana in his clothes (M)

2001-03-01.title: South Korea's New Best Friend?
2001-03-01.abstract: Analysis of South Korea's act of independence from Washington, tilting toward Russia on issue of American missile-defense program; South Korean officials say they fear another crisis over North Korea's ballistic missile program, and value Russia's increasingly important role in trying to persuade North Korean leader, Kim Jong Il, to give it up; photo of Pres Vladimir V Putin during visit to Seoul (M)

2001-03-01.title: 13 Die in British Train Crash, Adding to the Industry's Woes
2001-03-01.abstract: Thirteen people are killed and 70 injured when high-speed passenger train hits empty Land Rover stuck across track and then collides with freight train coming from opposite direction near North Yorkshire village of Great Heck; accident is latest in series of devastating incidents to hit Britain's railways; photo (M)

2001-03-02.title: Powell Goes on the Road And Scores Some Points
2001-03-02.abstract: Secretary of State Colin L Powell's just-completed first trip abroad reveals intensely personal style of diplomacy imbued with authority and confidence; Powell seems reassured that he was able to rally Arab leaders around new policy on Iraq that would tighten sanctions on military equipement and money and relax them on civilian goods; Powell acknowledged during trip that this policy has not been approved by Bush administration, but by traveling early and speaking out, he has put State Department squarely in favor of sanctions reform, not overthrow; several highlights of trip noted (M)

2001-03-02.title: Cartoon of Wartime 'Comfort Women' Irks Taiwan
2001-03-02.abstract: Chinese-language edition of Japanese comic book is causing storm in Taiwan over depiction of Taiwanese women who were kept in Japanese military brothels during World War II; mob of Taiwan legislators and other protesters recently stormed into Taipei's biggest bookstore, grabbed armloads of comics and set them on fire on sidewalk outside; critics are outraged by what they say is distorted account of Japan's last years in Taiwan, when army rounded up 'comfort women' for soldiers' use; cartoonist Kobayashi Yoshinori depicts women lining up willingly for duty, and to buttress his claims includes long interview with aging Taiwanese industrialist Hsu Wen-lung, who asserts that no women were forced to serve Japanese military; Hsu's remarks are particularly explosive because he is supporter and adviser to Pres Chen Shui-bian (M)

2001-03-02.title: ITALIAN FASHION : Mavericks With a Touch of Whimsy
2001-03-02.abstract: MILAN—  Lucia Sansalvadore, resembling a gothic heroine with her long brown tresses, slight figure and rosary beads, rose at sunrise in the Piedmont town of Asti, packed a van full of her clothing designs, and headed to the most fashionable boutiques in Milan, hoping for a break.

2001-03-02.title: DESPERATE MIGRATION -- A special report.; Fleeing Famine and War, Afghans Again Meet Death
2001-03-02.abstract: Special report on Afghanistan, where thousands of villagers are fleeing famine, many of them dying on journey to bigger towns and cities or borders of neighboring Iran and Pakistan; United Nations officials plea for emergency aid, saying more than million of people are at risk of starvation in country afflicted with unending war, no effectual government and three years of drought; photos of refugee camps; map (M)

2001-03-02.title: Upbeat Plan for a Dam in Belize Turns Nasty
2001-03-02.abstract: Plan for new dam along Macal River, which raised hopes in Belize of weaning itself from electricity bought from Mexico and providing new water source for existing dam and power plant that struggles during dry season, unleashes barrage of criticism from environmentalists; opponents, who say project would flood sensitive jungle habitat with several endangered species and dispute claim that dam would lower electricity rates, are garnering support of Natural Resources Defense Council and other international groups; map; photos (M)

2001-03-03.title: Tissue Tied to Mad Cow Is Found in Some Beef From the Continent : Risky Meat In Imports Angers U.K. Farmers
2001-03-03.abstract: PARIS—  British farmers, long used to seeing their beef banned in Europe, reacted angrily Friday to findings that meat imports from Germany and the Netherlands contained material most at risk from mad cow disease, and a leading agriculture spokesman in Brussels termed the fact "criminal."

2001-03-03.title: Foot-and-Mouth Disease Intrudes, Putting British Farmers in Dread
2001-03-03.abstract: British farmers fear for their futures as foot-and-mouth disease, highly contagious virus that attacks cloven-hoofed animals like cows, pigs and sheep, spreads; are barring visitors and leaving farms only when necessary; hunting, fishing and sports events have been cancelled to deter spread of virus, which rarely affects humans but can be carried by people, cars, clothes and even wind; photos (M)

2001-03-03.title: Oaxaca Journal; Mexican Rebels' Hopes Meet Hard Indian Reality
Putin, Ending Asia Trip, Appears Pleased With Renewed Stature
2001-03-03.abstract: Russian Pres Vladimir V Putin, ending visits to South Korea and Vietnam, seems pleased that he has re-established Russia as player in region; trip also shows how far Russia lags behind United States, Japan and China in playing meaningful Far East role (M)

2001-03-03.title: China Pursues Executions As Way to Cut Tax Swindles
2001-03-03.abstract: China sentences seven people to death for tax fraud, Guangdong, and executes two others for embezzlement, Guizhou Province; is in midst of wave of executions intended to curb corruption and economic crimes government blames for weakening economy and fears could threaten Communist Party's legitimacy (M)

2001-03-04.title: Iraqis Who Look Back in Anguish
2001-03-04.abstract: Iraqis like 66-year-old Hamza Jabbar are anguished at loss of good jobs, pensions and better lives they had in Kuwait, before they were expelled in days following Iraqi invasion of 1991; photo; map; Iraqi regime itself holds onto to proud pronouncements on 'joyous moment' of Saddam Hussein's invasion (M)

2001-03-04.title: We're Doing Battle Right and Left, Colombia Insists
2001-03-04.abstract: Colombian Pres Andres Pastrana, seeking more aid from US and Europe, vows to fight right-wing parramilitaries as well as leftist rebels, interview; some American officials and rights groups see little effort against paramilitary groups, which killed 577 people last year alone; photo (M)

2001-03-04.title: C. M. Woodhouse, Writer on Modern Greece, Dies at 83
2001-03-04.abstract: C M Woodhouse, writer on modern Greece, dies at age 83; photo (M)

2001-03-04.title: Global Look at Racism Hits Many Sore Points
2001-03-04.abstract: UN conference on racism, planned for this summer in South Africa, may be explosive, given moves afoot to cast globalization as racial issue and to demand reparations for slave trade and colonialism; meeting first proposed by Cuba was always expected to have anti-Western bias, but chance to air grievances has been seized by groups in developing countries from China to Chile (M)

2001-03-04.title: Over World Protests, Taliban Are Destroying Ancient Buddhas
2001-03-04.abstract: Afghanistan's Taliban officially announces ongoing systematic destruction of Great Buddhas of Bamiyan, priceless artifacts that survived wrathful cannon of Genghis Khan 800 years ago; supreme leader Mullah Muhammad Omar has ordered destruction of 'idols' that date to centuries befor Islam supplanted Buddhism, more than 1,000 years ago; international outcry results (M)

2001-03-05.title: China's Prime Minister Pledges to Promote Private Enterprise
2001-03-05.abstract: Beijing, March 5 - Prime Minister Zhu Rongji unveiled China's 10th Five-Year Plan today, promising that the country would forge ahead with the introduction of market forces and private enterprise. He said the Government would continue its program of large-scale investment's in the country's infrastructure and other forms of economic stimulus. He also predicted an annual growth rate of 7 percent in the years ahead, somewhat lower than in the previous five years.

2001-03-05.title: Moderate Jailed by Court in Iran
2001-03-05.abstract: TEHRAN, March 4 — A hard-line court has sentenced Iran's senior election official to jail for vote tampering, the state-run news media reported today, toppling one of the reformist president's top allies just three months before presidential elections.

2001-03-05.title: Swiss Voters Solidly Reject Talks On Joining the European Union
2001-03-05.abstract: Swiss voters resoundingly reject, by 77 percent margin, referendum calling for immediate negotiations on membership in European Union; government opposed referendum, calling it 'wrong question at the wrong time' (S)

2001-03-05.title: Norway's Thoroughly Modern Royal Pair
2001-03-05.abstract: Prince Haakon of Norway, country's hereditary prince, and 27-year-old lover Mette-Marit Tjessem Hoiby have become test for famously tolerant compatriots; Haakon will make her future queen in grand cathedral marriage in summer 2001, even though father of her three-year-old son is convicted cocaine supplier and she has well-known past in Oslo's dance-and-drugs house party scene; direct descendant of Queen Victoria was educated in public schools and received higher education not at Oxford, like grandparents, but at University of California at Berkeley, where he publicly reveled in society markedly more diverse than that in Scandinavia; photos (M)

2001-03-05.title: China's Prime Minister Pledges to Promote Private Enterprise
2001-03-05.abstract: Beijing, March 5 - Prime Minister Zhu Rongji unveiled China's 10th Five-Year Plan today, promising that the country would forge ahead with the introduction of market forces and private enterprise. He said the Government would continue its program of large-scale investment's in the country's infrastructure and other forms of economic stimulus. He also predicted an annual growth rate of 7 percent in the years ahead, somewhat lower than in the previous five years.

2001-03-06.title: Simple Aid to Gorillas Pays Off: Babies in the Bamboo
2001-03-06.abstract: Population of endangered mountain gorrillas in Volcano National Park in Rwanda, one of two places in world where they still exist, is inching up mainly because of modest conservation efforts to save them; recent preliminary estimate puts population at 358, up from 324 in 1989; scientists are pleased, given that numbers could easily have declined since park straddles Rwanda, Congo and Uganda--part of world with extraordinary amount of strife; important strategy of International Gorilla Conservation Program has been merely to keep park rangers paid and equipped in times of war so they can follow gorillas regularly and continue anti-poaching projects; maps; photo (M)

2001-03-06.title: Afghans Reject Appeals to Spare Statues
2001-03-06.abstract: Leader of Afghanistan's ruling Taliban movement rejects international appeals to spare ancient sculptures of Buddha deemed un-Islamic; says world is creating unnecessary 'drama' about campaign to destroy sculptures (S)

2001-03-06.title: Animals Set Afire as France Fears Outbreak
2001-03-06.abstract: Alarm that Britain's outbreak of foot-and-mouth disease may have crossed English Channel mounts after French officials report traces of virus in sheep on nine farms in five regions; government quickly halts meat exports and moves to complete slaughter of more than 50,000 animals that either came from Britain or came in contact with those that did; photo (M)

2001-03-06.title: Lisbon Blamed After a Plunge That Killed 60
2001-03-06.abstract: Residents of Castelo de Paiva, small Portuguese town, are in mourning for more than 60 people, killed on March 4 when bridge over Douro River collapsed, dropping crowded bus and two cars into raging waters; sorrow is mixed with anger; many say they have long complained to central government that 116-year-old bridge was unsafe, but that money to repair it was never given; public works minister, Jorge Coelho, resigns; Prime Min Antonio Guterres rejects criticisms of government during visit to assess damage (S)

2001-03-06.title: World Briefing
2001-03-06.abstract: ASIA    JAPAN: MORI SURVIVES VOTE -- Prime Minister Yoshiro Mori survived a no-confidence vote brought by the opposition. The governing Liberal Democratic Party, together with its coalition partners, blocked the motion in a 274-to-192 vote that largely followed party lines. Mr. Mori called the vote a show of confidence in his government. But with his own party members increasingly calling for his replacement, the vote was seen as little more than a face-saving reprieve for Mr. Mori, who is widely expected to quit soon.   Howard French (NYT)

2001-03-07.title: Korean to Visit Bush, but They Could Be at Odds
2001-03-07.abstract: Pres Jim Dae Jung of South Korea arrives, Washington, for meeting with Pres Bush; faces possible clash with administration over right strategy for dealing with North Korea as it emerges from self-imposed isolation for first time in nearly 50 years; plans by Kim to sign peace 'declaration' with North in coming months noted; decision comes as Bush administration is undertaking thorough review of American policy toward North Korea, with clear view toward taking far harder line than Clinton administration; senior administration officials continue to view North as major threat; are wary that Kim's peace initiatives have moved too fast, with too few concessions from North; photo (M)

2001-03-07.title: Warming Data:The Threat Can Now Be Tackled
2001-03-07.abstract: GENEVA—  Tackling the threat of global warming can be effectively started with existing technologies if governments decide to take action, a group of top climate-change experts says in a final report wrapping up the first comprehensive study of climate change data in five years.

2001-03-07.title: Taleban's Act Flies in Face of Islam's Tenets
2001-03-07.abstract: The destruction of two giant Buddhas carved out of a mountainside at Bamian, in the highlands of Afghanistan, is a disaster of abysmal proportions reminiscent of China's Cultural Revolution.

2001-03-07.title: 41 Killed After School in China Explodes
2001-03-07.abstract: SHANGHAI, March 7 — Rescue workers combed through the rubble of a school in central China today, looking for clues to an explosion Tuesday that killed 41 people, most of them children.

2001-03-07.title: South Korean President and Bush at Odds on North Korea
2001-03-07.abstract: WASHINGTON, March 7 - President Bush told President Kim Dae Jung of South Korea today that he would not resume negotiations with North Korea on missile talks anytime soon, putting the United States and a major Asian ally at sharp odds over how to deal with the communist regime.

2001-03-08.title: Behave or Face a Diamond Ban, Security Council Tells Liberians
2001-03-08.abstract: Security Council votes unanimously to bar all diamond exports from Liberia if country does not stop supporting rebel army that has ravaged neighboring Sierra Leone (M)

2001-03-08.title: NATO Soldiers Fire on Kosovo Albanians
2001-03-08.abstract: NATO soldiers in Kosovo open fire and wound two Albanian gunmen, raising prospect that peacekeeping force could become tangled in violent Albanian insurgency that has suddenly flared in neighboring Macedonia; peacekeepers are in command of multinational force that moved into Kosovo village of Mijak in operation to stem flow of arms and men to Albanian guerrillas in Macedonia, where clashes have erupted between ethnic Albanians and Macedonian military force; photo (M)

2001-03-08.title: Witness Ties Ex-Police Boss To Killing Of Warlord
2001-03-08.abstract: Rade Markovic, Serbia's former secret police chief, and several other senior police officers who served under Pres Slobodan Milosevic will be summoned as witness in trial in Belgrade of 10 men accused of killing Arkan, notorious paramilitary leader; Vojislav Jekic, former police chief of Loznica, testifies that two of defendants told him that group was responsible for killings and that it was backed by Markovic and a wealthy businessman (M)

2001-03-08.title: NATO Troops Help Macedonians Drive Away Ethnic Albanian Rebels
2001-03-08.abstract: SKOPJE, Macedonia, March 8 — United States-led peacekeeping troops in southern Kosovo occupied a village on the border with Macedonia today, as part of a coordinated move with Macedonian military forces through the night and morning to flush out ethnic Albanian rebels from their mountain base.

2001-03-08.title: Explosion Kills 41 in School in Central China
2001-03-08.abstract: Explosion in school in Wanzai, China, kills 41 people, mostly children, who reportedly have been assembling fireworks in a business set up by head teacher and local officials; officials deny reports that blast went off during assembly of fireworks, dominant industry in impoverished region; map (M)

2001-03-09.title: Argentine Court Ruling Could Open the Military to Prosecution
2001-03-09.abstract: Ruling by federal Judge Gabril Cavallo to overturn two laws granting immunity to hundreds of military officers comes as another sign that human rights law is gaining new force in Argentina; ruling is expected to lead to new criminal complaints against military commanders, perhaps including current army chief Gen Ricardo Brinzoni, who served in 1976-83 military dictatorship that was involved in killings of more than 15,000 people case is expected to put Pres Fernando de la Rua in awkward

2001-03-09.title: Worldwide, There Remains a 'Long Way to Go' in Combating Inequality : Rallying Cries on a Sober Women's Day
2001-03-09.abstract: GENEVA—  On the day set aside each year for women to celebrate their progress and achievements, there were some sober notes Thursday reminding women that while they are increasingly able to support themselves, they still face discrimination and even violence in the workplace, in public life and at home.

2001-03-09.title: American Topics : Chores:A Thing of the Past
2001-03-09.abstract: Throughout the United States, chores are becoming as obsolete as hi-fi turntables. The amount of time 9- to 12-year-olds spent doing housework declined 30 percent from 1981 to 1997, from an average of 5 hours a week to 3 hours, 42 minutes a week, according to a University of Michigan researcher.

2001-03-09.title: G.I.'s Join Macedonians in Fight Against Albanian Rebels
2001-03-09.abstract: Largely American peacekeeping force goes on offensive against Albanian rebels, occupying village of Tanusevci on Macedonia-Kosovo border in coordinated move with Macedonia to flush guerrillas from their mountain base; operation by some 300 peacekeepers is most powerful thrust to date against Albanian gunmen moving between Macedonia and Kosovo; NATO's original mission in Kosovo was to drive Serbian forces out and police the province, but major challenge now is armed Albanians who have taken control of border areas inside Serbia and are also active in Macedonia; map; photo (M)

2001-03-09.title: U.S. Won't Send Its Kosovo G.I.'s On Peacekeeping Patrol in Serbia
2001-03-09.abstract: Bush administration tells NATO allies that it will not let American peacekeepers cross from Kosovo into Serbia proper despite spreading Albanian insurgency; Britain and other allies have urged more assertive action to monitor Albanian insurgents in border zone in Serbia from which NATO has excluded Yugoslav Army; administration concern that expanded mission would embroil US in wider Balkans conflict comes even as largely American force of peacekeepers takes its strongest action to date against Albanians in Macedonia, where an insurgency has also sprung up; troops act to support Macedonian government, which fears an open rebellion that could foment its Albanian minority and cause wider conflict; photo (M)

2001-03-10.title: Leaders' Cordiality Doesn't Halt Gaza Clashes
2001-03-10.abstract: Israeli Prime Min Ariel Sharon has told Yasir Arafat that he hopes they can establish 'personal contact' after violence diminishes in region, but prospects for meeting any time soon appear remote as street clashes and gun battles flare anew throughout Gaza and West Bank; photo (M)

2001-03-10.title: Africa's AIDS War
2001-03-10.abstract: News analysis of AIDS epidemic in Africa; says now that world's biggest pharmaceutical companies have been forced into painful negotiations over patient rights and price of medicines, question suddenly is no longer whether Africans will get life-saving drug cocktails, but how; many African nations are too poor to afford medicines, even at steeply discounted prices, and some lack infrastructure to deliver drugs and educate patients about how to use them (M)

2001-03-10.title: A Tale of China's Harsh and Secretive Justice
2001-03-10.abstract: SHANGHAI, March 10 — When Qiu Xuanming's body arrived at the crematory from the execution ground last June, his brother recognized it by the clothes it wore. Mr. Qiu's head, which had been shot in the back at close range, was a tightly wrapped ball of white gauze, but the clothes were the same he had worn at his final court hearing a little more than an hour before.

2001-03-10.title: Palestinians Delicately Begin Debate on Circle of Violence
2001-03-10.abstract: AL BIREH, West Bank, March 8 — Muhammad Darraj, a 40-year-old construction worker with weathered hands and dirt-caked boots, moved his family into a new apartment with a commanding view of a Jewish settlement several weeks ago. His reasons were pedestrian but loomed large in his life: he wanted to own, not rent.

2001-03-10.title: Japan's Chief Sends Signal He's Prepared to Step Down
2001-03-10.abstract: TOKYO, March 10 — After a week of resignation rumors and steady denials, Prime Minister Yoshiro Mori met privately with senior members of his party today to signal his intention to step down early next month.

2001-03-11.title: Stone Age Ways Surviving, Barely
2001-03-11.abstract: Dani tribe, largest ethnic group in Irian Jaya, Indonesia, lives much as its ancestors did, six decades after Western explorers arrived; but ancient farming village of Anemaugi faces church and government pressures to change its ways; tribal leaders and anthropologists express concern that growing number of Dani young people are abandoning tribe's traditions and animist beliefs for modern conveniences and Christianity; they blame mounting pressure fromp government and missionaries, who have tried hard to bring Dani into modern world, as well as recent influx of migrants from other parts of Indonesia and foreign tourists; photos; map (L)

2001-03-11.title: Election Bringing Out New Uganda's Rough Edges
2001-03-11.abstract: Uganda Pres Yoweri Museveni faces election challenge; Dr Kizza Besigye is his main opponent; compared with election campaigns in other African countries, Uganda's election has had relatively contained violence; but given Museveni's accomplishments since he took office in 1986, disappointments run deep; and given Uganda's past, violence has scared many Ugandans; photos (L)

2001-03-11.title: Canada and British Columbia's Largest Indian Group Taking Steps to First Permanent Treaty
2001-03-11.abstract: Members of largest tribal group in British Columbia sign their first treaty with provincial and federal governments; treaty gives Indians--7,000 members of Nuu-chah-nulth Tribal Council--self-rule, a large cash payment and shared control with non-Indians of spectacular old-growth forests and other natural resources; map (M)

2001-03-11.title: Arafat Stops Short of Rejecting Violence
2001-03-11.abstract: Yasir Arafat, in his first major speech since violence erupted last fall, reaffirms his commitment to 'strategic path of peace' but stops short of calling for end to violence as a component of Palestinian uprising; addresses first full meeting of Palestinian legislative council in more than five months, Gaza City; photo (M)

2001-03-11.title: Some Hopeful Signs as Colombia Meets With Rebels and Envoys
2001-03-11.abstract: Colombia rebels, who have been cold to idea of foreign participation in peace talks, agree to meet with envoys from 25 countries, move that is seen encouraging sign; rebels agree that small group of nations could serve as facilitators in future peace negotiations (M)a

2001-03-12.title: Ugandans Casting Their Votes Today for President
2001-03-12.abstract: KATETE, Uganda, March 12 — Voting today in Uganda was far more peaceful than the last few weeks of campaigning, but at least here, problems lingered: Felix Hakiza, holding his 4-year-old son by the hand, showed up to vote this afternoon only to find that someone else had done it for him.

2001-03-12.title: Zapatista Rebels Rally in Mexico City
2001-03-12.abstract: Mexico's Zapatista rebels and leader Subcommander Marcos end two-week trek from jungle hideout in Chiapas with massive demonstration in capital of Mexico City; Marcos addresses throng, estimated at 100,000; demands democracy, liberty and justice for nation's minority Indian population of about 10 million; disproportionately poor, illiterate, malnourished and disenfranchised, they have been traditionally treated as second-class citizens; photos (M)

2001-03-12.title: Washington Memo; Divergent Voices Heard In Bush Foreign Policy
2001-03-12.abstract: Washington Memo on gap in tone between Pres Bush and Sec of State Colin L Powell; says it raises questions of whose voice is dominant in foreign policy and in longer term which opinions will prevail; early disagreements could put factions within Republican Party at odds with one another, leaving Bush to pick policy and risk alienating some faction and possible overseas allies; early disagreements over Iraq and North Korea discussed (M)

2001-03-12.title: Syria Reaches Turning Point, But Which Way Will It Turn?
2001-03-12.abstract: Syria is living through heady period of ferment, shifting from repressive and stagnant socialized state created by late president Hafez al-Assad, to reign of son Bashar al-Assad and outcome remains uncertain; change is in keeping with Bashar's promise to transform country, fostering more openness and robust economy, but has been more in tone than substance; daily life remains unchanged and long-dominant Baath Party easily ressurects oppressive measures whenever it deems denunciations of government to be too harsh; even though Bashar intimates he wants freer country, question remains how free; general sense is that he seeks change along lines of China, unfettering economy while Baath Party hold political reins firmly; photo (M)

2001-03-12.title: 2 Stubborn Factions Divide Coming U.N. Meeting on Racism
2001-03-12.abstract: Four-day meeting in Geneva, aimed at bringing some coherence to agenda of an UN conference on racism in August, ends with virtually every major issue still in dispute; European countries and bloc of Asian, African and Latin American nations said to be increasingly divided over what conference will address (M)

2001-03-13.title: A U.N. Paradox: Some on Rights Panel Are Accused of Wrongs
2001-03-13.abstract: Human rights groups say UN Human Rights Commission, focal point of debate among nations on range of political and civil liberties, is growing steadily less free and tolerant in its membership; say makeup of commission, whose members are elected for staggered three-year terms, is becoming problematic with each passing year; recent ranking of commission members found approximately half represent governments with repressive human rights policies; some members bar human rights monitors from visiting their countries (S)

2001-03-13.title: Colombian Governors Protest U.S.-Backed Spraying of Coca
2001-03-13.abstract: Four Colombian governors charge American-supported aerial spraying of illicit coca crops is jeopardizing health and food supply of small-scale farmers; are urging Pres Andres Pastrana and Bush administration to rethink what they maintain is militaristic approach toward peasant farmers, in exchange for plan involving social pacts and alternative crops in southern drug-growing states that they represent (M)

2001-03-13.title: Putin to Sell Arms and Nuclear Help to Iran
2001-03-13.abstract: Russia's Pres Vladimir V Putin breaks openly with US and predecessor Boris N Yeltsin, formally agreeing to resume sales of conventional arms to Iran after hiatus of more than five years; reiterates at Kremlin meeting with Pres Mohammad Khatami Russia's intention to help Iran complete long-stalled nuclear power plant some American experts contend could advance Iran's nuclear weapons program; announcements, neither unexpected, come during first meeting in four decades between Iranian and Russian heads of state in warm session billed as diplomatic turning point; meeting is seen as pointed signal to Bush administration that Iranians and Russians intend to limit American influence in Middle East by both diplomatic and military means; Russia sold approximately $5 billion in weapons to Iran from 1989 and 1995, when sales stopped under terms of accord signed secretly with US by former Pres Boris Yeltsin; photo (M)

2001-03-13.title: Sharon Blockades a Palestinian Center in the West Bank
2001-03-13.abstract: Israeli Army seals off Ramallah, unofficial seat of government of Palestinian Authority in West Bank; blocks roads with trenches, mounds of earth and checkpoints backed by tanks and armored troop carriers; action is first against Palestinians by newly inaugurated government of Prime Min Ariel Sharon and is most severe blockade imposed by Israelis on major commercial and cultural community of 60,000 people; Palestinians officials condemn action as collective punishment; Sharon denies blockade represents new policy; says it is imposed to foil car bombing in Jerusalem following arrest of members of group planning attack; says policy is directed at attackers and their accomplices; Defense Min Binyamin Ben-Eliezer orders lifting of less stringent blockades imposed by previous government on four other West Bank cities; photo; map (M)

2001-03-13.title: North Korea Abruptly Cancels Meetings with South
2001-03-13.abstract: SEOUL, March 13 — North Korea postponed without warning today a series of meetings planned for this week between North and South Korean cabinet members on major topics affecting inter-Korean relations.

2001-03-14.title: MEAT FROM EUROPE IS BANNED BY U.S. AS ILLNESS SPREADS
2001-03-14.abstract: US bans imports of animals and animal products from European Union countries in response to spread of foot-and-mouth disease from Britain to France; photos; virus poses little danger to people but is virulently contagious and devastating among cloven-hoofed animals; Belgium, Portugal, Spain, Switzerland and other countries take parallel measures; Canada bans meat from Argentina as well because disease has been found there; Agriculture Department explains that most cheeses and cured or cooked meats are not affected because heating preparation kills virus; pork producers are most affected since most European beef is already banned by US because of mad cow disease (M)

2001-03-14.title: Fund for Former Slaves of Third Reich Tops $2.5 Billion
2001-03-14.abstract: FRANKFURT, March 14 — A foundation set up by German industry announced on Tuesday that it had raised nearly $2.5 billion to compensate former slave workers during the Third Reich, but executives said today they will not release the money until all lawsuits pending in the United States have been dismissed.

2001-03-14.title: Foot-and-Mouth's Harsh Approach
2001-03-14.abstract: Western European countries have chosen to deal with foot-and-mouth disease largely by slaughter and cremation during rare outbreaks, rather than by inoculating animals as many other countries, including some in Eastern Europe, do; some British scientists argue that killing huge numbers is senseless because 80-95 percent will survive disease; urge return to pre-1991 practice of inoculation (S)

2001-03-14.title: Under Pressure, Israel Eases Blockade a Bit at West Bank City
2001-03-14.abstract: Israel eases tight blockade of Ramallah, opening two roads on West Bank and allowing some Palestinians through after security checks (S)

2001-03-14.title: Pilot Given Wrong Site For Bombs In Kuwait
2001-03-14.abstract: WASHINGTON—  A U.S. serviceman on the ground at a firing range in Kuwait mistakenly directed a pilot to drop his bombs over the site where he and other observers were watching a routine exercise, then sought frantically, but in vain, to call off the bombing, American officials said Tuesday.

2001-03-15.title: As the Disease Marches On, Britain Dooms More Animals
2001-03-15.abstract: Confirmation of 27 new cases of foot-and-mouth disease brings total in Britain to 232 and prompts Prime Min Tony Blair to order intensified slaughter of animals in areas directly affected; he also says government should consider easing restrictions caused by disease in economically hard-hit regions that are free of virus; says difficulty is weighing any relaxation of rules with need to make sure government is taking right precautions; Australia, which earlier banned importing horses from Britain, expands its ban to include all livestock from Europe (M)

2001-03-15.title: North Korea Turns Up the Heat; Calls U.S. a Nation of Cannibals
2001-03-15.abstract: North Korea calls United States 'a cannibals' nation' and charges Bush administration with escalating provocative and reckless diatribe against North; pledges 'thousandfold revenge' if United States turns to confrontation; North has been ratcheting up its criticism since Pres Bush, in talks with South Korean president, Kim Dae Jung, expressed 'skepticism' about North Korean leader, Kim Jong Il (M)

2001-03-15.title: Japan's Affair With Bay Area
2001-03-15.abstract: SAN FRANCISCO—  This is what the Japanese are discovering about the San Francisco Bay area: It almost feels like home. Home without the kinks of societal and corporate pressures, frenetic pace (and damnable cost) of living. Home plus leafy tree-lined streets and trolley cars and coffee that comes in stupendously large cups.

2001-03-15.title: Bush Plans to Visit China in October
2001-03-15.abstract: WASHINGTON, March 15 — Administration officials said today that President Bush intends to make a state visit to China in October, but the White House held off on an official announcement of the visit even though Chinese Prime Minister Zhu Rongji prematurely mentioned it in Beijing.

2001-03-15.title: Final Count Has Uganda President Winning 69% of Vote
2001-03-15.abstract: Pres Yoweri Museveni sweeps hard-fought elections in Uganda, winning 69.3 percent of vote; his main challenger, Kizza Besigye, takes 27.8 percent and four other candidates split rest; Museveni calls his victory acclamation of 15 years of peaceful rule; Besigye accuses him of extensive cheating and vote rigging; weeks leading up to election were fraught with incidents of violence, disturbing to nation so eager to transcend its turbulent past; photo (M)

2001-03-16.title: Blasts in China Leave at Least 18 Dead
2001-03-16.abstract: SHANGHAI, March 16 — A series of apparently coordinated blasts killed at least 18 people in an industrial city 120 miles south of Beijing early Friday, sending riot police into the streets and sparking rumors that laid-off factory workers were wreaking revenge.

2001-03-16.title: Shenzhen Lures Savvy Shoppers
2001-03-16.abstract: HONG KONG—  Hong Kong is still a magnet for the born-to-consume crowd, but now the main attraction is a shopping mall just across the border in mainland China.

2001-03-16.title: Foot-and-Mouth Damages English Tourism, Too
2001-03-16.abstract: Outbreak of foot-and-mouth disease is taking its toll on entire regions of England where hoteliers, shopkeepers and suppliers depend on visitors for their livelihoods; much of Britain has been placed off limits to walkers and outsiders for fear they may carry disease to infect new animals; many hotels are all but empty, roads are free of tourists' cars and delivery vans, and employees in tourism business are being laid off; photo (M)

2001-03-16.title: Albanian Rebels Widen Attack Along Macedonia Border
2001-03-16.abstract: Police troops in Tetovo, Macedonia's second-largest city, battle ethnic Albanian rebels at edge of town for two days, and there have been some civilian casualties; fighting demonstrates how Albanians are intensifying their activity in Macedonia, even as Albanian rebels in southern Serbia, east of Kosovo, enter negotiations to end year of conflict there; insurgents are trying to dominate border areas in Macedonia where residents are overwhelmingly Albanian; photo; map (M)

2001-03-16.title: China Backs Away From Initial Denial In School Explosion
2001-03-16.abstract: Prime Min Zhu Rongji makes highly unusual nationally televised apology to people of China for deadly schoolhouse explosion in rural Jiangxi Province, and softens earlier denials that children at school were making fireworks; says he has ordered high-level investigation; government's attempts to contain conflicting accounts of blast were undermined by citizens' rapidly spreading access to Internet and other information channels and by increasingly self-assertive press; explosion killed 42 people, 38 of them children aged 9 to 11 years old; local government had announced that deranged suicide bomber was responsible for blast; bereaved parents and surviving students unanimously rejected this, saying children were forced to make fireworks at school; attempted media blackout only fueled speculation that there was coverup reaching into central government; photo (M)

2001-03-17.title: Ego Bashing And Scandals As Paris Picks A New Mayor
2001-03-17.abstract: Battle over Paris mayoralty continues to provide twists, turns, name-calling, bruised egos and distinct possibility that conservatives will lose control of capital for first time in more than century; Parisians are disgusted with corruption charges that plague right, and bewildered by spectacle of conservative candidates insulting each other; many pollsters believe voters may give Socialists' candidate, Bertrand Delanoe, a narrow victory; current mayor, Jean Tiberi, who had been handpicked for job by Pres Jacques Chirac, has become subject of growing number of investigations; Tiberi has refused to step aside for Philippe Seguin, chosen by his party to run for mayor; photo (M)

2001-03-17.title: World Briefing
2001-03-17.abstract: THE AMERICAS    BRAZIL: SINKING OIL RIG  The world's biggest offshore oil rig, hit by a series of blasts on Thursday that killed at least one person and left nine missing, could sink within days as the chances of recovery fade, said the president of the state oil company, Petrobras. Engineers said the sinking of the 40-story rig could cause an environmental catastrophe as oil spills into the sea. The cause of the explosion is still unknown.   (Reuters)    ASIA    INDIA: ARMS SCANDAL INQUIRY  The cabinet authorized a Supreme Court judge to begin an investigation into allegations of high-level corruption that came to light in a sting operation by journalists pretending to be arms dealers. For the fourth day, the opposition forced the adjournment of Parliament in protest over the scandal, but the governing coalition rallied around Prime Minister Atal Behari Vajpayee.    Celia W. Dugger (NYT)

2001-03-17.title: Chtaura Journal; In a Foreign Town, Syrians (Shhh!) Bank and Buy
2001-03-17.abstract: Chtaura, Lebanon, just 10 miles from Syrian border, has prospered even during years of civil war that made Beirut unlivable; Syrian citizens visit Chtaura in droves to stash their illegal dollars away from prying eyes in Lebanese banks, to get credit cards that Syria's state-controlled banks do not issue, and to shop for delicacies, like real Coca-Cola, not available or forbidden in their own country; bankers and merchants of Chtaura worry that boom may end now that Hafez al-Assad has died and been succeeded by his son, Bashar al-Assad, who has started relaxing all kinds of rules in Syria; photo; map (M)

2001-03-17.title: Blasts Kill 18 In Textile City South of Beijing
2001-03-17.abstract: Apparently coordinated explosions kill at least 18 people in Shijiazhuang, industrial city 120 miles south of Beijing, send riot police into streets and spark rumors of laid-off textile workers and others seeking revenge; map (S)

2001-03-17.title: Saudi Arabian Police Raid Hijacked Russian Jetliner
2001-03-17.abstract: Saudi Arabian police officers force their way into hijacked Russian jet, freeing more than 100 passengers and shooting dead one hijacker after he fatally stabs flight attendant; Turkish passenger is also reported killed in raid; plane was seized by three or four armed men as it took off from Istanbul bound for Moscow; it landed in western Saudi city of Medina; Saudi and Russian officials say hijackers are from rebellious Russian republic of Chechnya; Pres Vladimir V Putin reportedly gave his consent to Saudi authorities to storm plane; Russia's Foreign Ministry asks Saudi authorities to return hijackers to Russia, although two countries have no extradition treaty (M)

2001-03-18.title: Irina Bugrimova, 90, an Enchanter of Big Cats
2001-03-18.abstract: Irina Bugrimova, Russian circus animal tamer, dies at age 90; photo (M)

2001-03-18.title: Less Patient, Japan Awaits A Transition
2001-03-18.abstract: Rank and file in Japan's Liberal Democratic Party signal that they will no longer entrust transition matters to party's elderly barons when Prime Min Yoshiro Mori steps down; demand that voting for next party president be opened to rank and file for first time; they are stepping up pressure, publicly voicing their discontent with their own party with passion and directness rare in Japan's usually muted politics; some local chapters are even calling for direct election of prime minister; it is not clear how far revolt can go, but open airing of discontent has prompted one major concession from leadership: starting in September, local chapters will be allowed to vote in party leadership elections (M)

2001-03-18.title: Great Alzana, Who Mocked Danger and New York Law on the High Wire, Dies at 82
2001-03-18.abstract: Harold Davis, known as The Great Alanza, high-wire daredevil whose act inspired New York's safety net law for aerial acts, dies at age 82; photo (M)

2001-03-18.title: Colonel's Trial Puts Russian Justice to Test
2001-03-18.abstract: Colonel's trial in killing of young Chechen woman puts Russian justice to test; Col Yuri D Budanov, highest-ranking officer to be accused of crime against Chechen civilian, admits that he abducted, beat and killed 18-year-old Elza Kungayeva in Grozny, Chechnya's capital, year ago; claims he believed she was rebel sniper who had killed soldiers in his regiment, and that he killed her in uncontrollable fit of revenge; woman's father disputes this, saying Budanov had 'noticed' his daughter in days before abduction; initial accusation of rape, documented by autopsy, has vanished; Human Rights Watch has documented thousands of cases in which Russian military killed, tortured and raped Chechen civilians with no punishment; despite fact that Kremlin itself expressed outrage at Kungayeva's killing, half of Russians polled feel Budanov should be set free; photos; map (M)

2001-03-18.title: Suspect Sought In China Blasts; Toll Is at 108
2001-03-18.abstract: Newspapers in Shijiazhuang, China, carry rare front-page wanted notice for Jin Ruchao, reportedly a suspect in Mar 16 coordinated explosions in four buildings that shook city and caused 108 deaths; Jin lives in one of destroyed buildings and is aready wanted for murder in death of woman several weeks ago; photo (M)

2001-03-19.title: As a Russian Hurries Home, Washington Is Suspicious
2001-03-19.abstract: Federal Bureau of Investigation identifies Vladimir Frolov, Russian press attache in Washington who abruptly cut short second tour of duty in US, as spy who may have handled Richard P Hanssen, agency operative who has been charged with spying for Russia; officials say Frolov was not forced to leave by US; speculate departure may have been part of pre-emptive move by Russian government in anticipation of Bush administration demands that some of its intelligence officers leave (M)

2001-03-19.title: U.S. and Japan Make Pledge to Collaborate on Economy
2001-03-19.abstract: WASHINGTON, March 19 — President Bush and Japan's weakened prime minister, Yoshiro Mori, met today to discuss joint strategies for restoring growth to the world's largest and second-largest economies, but ended the session with a vague commitment by Japan to confront its huge banking problem and not to seek a short-term solution by boosting exports to the United States.

2001-03-19.title: Gulf Arab States Prepare a Bailout to Assist Arafat
2001-03-19.abstract: JERUSALEM, March 19 — Persian Gulf oil states are tossing a lifeline to the financially foundering Palestinian Authority in the form of a $300 million aid package. This will make Arab donors the Authority's chief source of budgetary support for the first time.

2001-03-19.title: BUSH DUE TO MEET CHINESE ON ISSUES CRUCIAL FOR TIES
2001-03-19.abstract: Pres Bush prepares for meeting with China's Deputy Prime Min Qian Qichen in Washington, first with senior Chinese official, as administration faces crucial decisions on military issues that could quickly put US on collision course with Beijing; issues are whether to design national missile defense capable of countering China's small nuclear force or whether to sell Aegis destroyers to Taiwan; China's concern that Aegis system could be used as platform for missile shield for Taiwan and usher in new level of American military cooperation with island, regarded as renegade province, noted; importance of issues among conservative Republicans, who are deeply suspicious of Beijing and who represent important Bush constituency, discussed (M)

2001-03-19.title: Rebels Hope to Shift Borders in Balkans, Diplomats Say
2001-03-19.abstract: Senior Western diplomats say ethnic Albanians attacking Macedonian and Serbian governments are hoping to force international community into another diplomatic intervention that will alter borders in Balkans; say West has no intention of calling new international conference, like 1995 Dayton meeting that ended Bosnian war, or of blessing violent shifting of borders; photo (M)

2001-03-20.title: Rights Chief At U.N. Says She'll Leave In September
2001-03-20.abstract: Mary Robinson, UN high commissioner for human rights, says she will step down when term ends in September 2001 and will carry on fight for human rights without 'constraints' imposed by organization; criticisms and controversies that have marked her tenure discussed (M)

2001-03-20.title: GULF ARAB STATES PREPARE A BAILOUT TO ASSIST ARAFAT
2001-03-20.abstract: Persian Gulf oil states toss lifeline to financially foundering Palestinian Authority in form of $300 million aid package, making Arab donors its chief source of budgetary support for first time; Islamic Development Bank, under plan devised with support of International Monetary Fund, will provide up to $40 million per month for six months from new 'intifada fund;' most of money will come from Saudi Arabia, United Emirates and Kuwait, which previously refused to privide direct aid to semiautonomous government; payments are in addition to unpublicized transfer of $80 million in Arab grants and loans to authority since December 2000, exceeding $65 million received from European governments; photo (M)

2001-03-20.title: On the Front in Macedonia: A Show of Rebels' Tenacity
2001-03-20.abstract: Intense fighting between Albanian rebels and government forces near Tetovo, Macedonia, described; Albanians appear to have arms, ammunition and men they need in drive taking shape around country's second-largest town to take over large areas of Albanian populations; Macedonian Army deploys tanks and truckoads of troops in and around area for 'final operation' to rout rebels, who have grown from handful of fighters reportedly to force of several hundred; map; photo (M)

2001-03-20.title: Tran Van Lam, 88, Top South Vietnam Aide
2001-03-20.abstract: Tran Van Lam, former foreign minister of South Vietnam, dies at age 88; photo (S)

2001-03-20.title: Indian Rights Bill Falters in Mexico; Rebel Going Home
2001-03-20.abstract: Subcommander Marcos, leader of Mexico's Zapatista rebels who traveled to capital in hopes of achieving political settlement with government, says he will return to jungle in Chiapas; decision follows failure of efforts to pass measure that would give some autonomy to indigenous Indian communities (S)

2001-03-21.title: Moscow Says Remarks by U.S. Resurrect 'Spirit of Cold War'
2001-03-21.abstract: Russian Foreign Ministry scores as 'openly confrontational' remarks by United States Defense Sec Donald Rumsfeld and his deputy, Paul D Wolfowitz, in British newspaper interview complaining of Russia's role in providing missile technology to Iran and other nations; interview comes at time when Bush administration weighs possibly down-grading ties with Russia (M)

2001-03-21.title: President of Mexico Does Not Stand on Ceremony
2001-03-21.abstract: Pres Vicente Fox, in break with aloof style of past Mexican presidents, is mixing warmly with public, making frequent speeches and putting his Catholic faith on display; his romance with Martha Sahagun, his chief spokeswoman, is frequent fodder for newspaper columns; Fox says he seeks to be closer to people, interview; photo (M)

2001-03-21.title: Macedonia Gives Rebels an Ultimatum
2001-03-21.abstract: Macedonia threatens to wipe out ethnic Albanian rebels who occupy hills above Tetovo unless they surrender or withdraw in 24 hours; rebel commander rejects ultimatum; army has begun intensive drive against rebels, using tanks and artillery for first time in weeklong fighting in and around city; Albanian leader Arben Xhaferi threatens to pull out of multi-ethnic governing coalition if civilians are hurt or artillery is used against civilian targets, news conference; he says he opposes offensive and his party was not included in decision to launch it; scores rebels and their use of force; hails accord among all Albanian parties rejecting violence and appealing for peace; map; photo (M)

2001-03-21.title: English Village Butchery Blamed for Mad Cow Deaths
2001-03-21.abstract: LONDON, March 21 - A report into five deaths from the human form of mad cow disease in the same English village concluded today that the cause was a traditional local method of slaughter that permitted brain matter from diseased animals to contaminate cuts of meat.

2001-03-21.title: World Briefing
2001-03-21.abstract: AFRICA    UGANDA: EX-CANDIDATE QUESTIONED -- The police questioned the loser in last week's presidential race over a spate of attacks that have killed more than a dozen people around the country since the election. The former candidate, Dr. Kizza Besigye, left, who was President Yoweri Museveni's physician, denied any involvement in the attacks, saying the government is harassing him because of his accusations that Mr. Museveni won only by wholesale vote rigging. Last weekend, army officials prevented Dr. Besigye from traveling to South Africa because of what they said were suspicions that he may have been linked to the attacks.    Ian Fisher (NYT)

2001-03-22.title: Russia Warns It Will Reply in Kind to Ouster of Envoys
2001-03-22.abstract: MOSCOW, March 22 — Russia said today that the expulsion of 50 Russian diplomats accused of spying in the United States was a hostile act that would lead to the expulsion of American diplomats here.

2001-03-22.title: Europeans Urge Macedonia to Use Restraint With Rebels
2001-03-22.abstract: TETOVO, Macedonia, March 22 — Top European Union officials urged the Macedonian government today to exercise restraint against ethnic Albanian militants and to intensify discussions with elected Albanian politicians about political change.

2001-03-22.title: Iraqis Now Daring to Talk of Life After Hussein
2001-03-22.abstract: Growing numbers of Iraqis are ready to take risk of whispering their fear of Saddam Hussein and deep-seated hatred they say he has engendered among country's 23 million people; even officials at senior levels are beginning to speak of time when Hussein will no longer be country's ruler; some even allude to his eventual death; decade ago, suggesting that Hussein might be mortal was unthinkable in Iraq; there are rumors that Hussein is ill; Hussein has offered no public denial of this, and he has kept up vigorous schedule of official duties; Central Intelligence Agency has been dismissive of such rumors; photo (M)

2001-03-22.title: Richard Li's Company Removes Web Site Reference to Degree From Stanford : Hong Kong Tycoon's 'Diploma' Disappears
2001-03-22.abstract: HONG KONG—  The Hong Kong Internet tycoon Richard Li has made headlines as an Asian technology wizard whose career merited comparison with that of Bill Gates.

2001-03-22.title: Skull May Alter Experts' View Of Human Descent's Branches
2001-03-22.abstract: Paleontologists in Africa say 3.5 million-year-old skull they have found is from entirely new branch of early human family tree; discovery threatens to overturn prevailing view that single line of descent stretches through early stages of human ancestry; it now seems that fossil species Australopithecus afarensis, which lived four million to three million years ago and is best known from celebrated Lucy skeleton, was not alone on African plain, and that Lucy may not even be direct human ancestor after all; Dr Maeve G Leakey, who led research team that made new skull discovery in 1999, reports in journal Nature that skull's flattened face and small molars are strikingly different from those of Lucy species; Leakey has named new member of hominid family Kenyanthropus platyops, or flat-faced man of Kenya; photos; chart (M)

2001-03-23.title: China Says U.S. Scholar 'Confessed Her Crimes'
2001-03-23.abstract: Chinese government spokesman says Gao Zhan, United States-based sociologist who has been held incommunicado since Feb 11 for unspecified 'activities damaging state security,' has confessed her crimes; refuses to say what she actually confessed to (M)

2001-03-23.title: Bay of Pigs Enemies Finally Sit Down Together
2001-03-23.abstract: Pres Fidel Castro meets for first time with veterans of Bay of Pigs, doomed invasion of Cuba by anti-Castro forces backed by White House and Central Intelligence Agency 40 years ago; meeting, in Havana, opens three-day conference organized by American historians, scholars and open-government advocates who have helped to force access to secret archives of American, Cuban and other governments; Castro reads with delight from declassified State Dept report from 1959 calling him 'born leader of great personal courage and conviction'; this is first time Cuban exiles who fought Castro's forces at Bay of Pigs have returned to Cuba; CIA's brigade of 1,400 Cuban exiles was crushed by Castro's forces: 114 died and 1,189 were captured; photo; map (M)

2001-03-23.title: Mir Space Station Sizzles to Ending Over Pacific
2001-03-23.abstract: Mir space station streaks back to Earth in controlled descent, ending 15 years as orbiting laboratory for Soviet and then Russian science; Russian space agency at Mission Control near Moscow handles descent with remarkable precision; witnesses on Fiji report spectacular display of gold and silver lights streaming across sky as Mir crashes into ocean about 1,800 miles east of New Zealand; efforts to save Mir by turning it into movie set, tourist lodge or venue for television series came too late; photo (M)

2001-03-23.title: Europe Union Asks Restraint By Macedonia
2001-03-23.abstract: Top European Union officials urge Macedonian government to exercise restraint against ethnic Albanian militants and to step up discussions with elected Albanian politicians about political change; in response, Macedonian government appears to pull back from vow to 'neutralize the armed terrorists' before serious political discussions; photo (M)

2001-03-23.title: Russia Calls Expulsions by U.S. Hostile Act and Vows to Retaliate
2001-03-23.abstract: Russian government denounces expulsion of about 50 of its diplomats from United States as 'hostile' act and vows 'adequate' retaliation; Foreign Min Igor S Ivanov makes clear that equal number of Americans will soon be expelled from Russia; expulsions, exchanges and threats revive cold war tone that has been largely absent between Washington and Moscow since Soviet Union disintegrated in 1991, and signals marked departure by Bush administration from Pres Clinton's policies of engagement with Russia; American officials say four Russians ordered out immediately had been involved in handling Robert Philip Hanssen, FBI agent who has been charged with spying for Moscow for many years; say other 46 are Russian intelligence officers; say Washington station chief of Russian intelligence service has not been expelled because he operates openly and was not directly involved in Hanssen case; photo; chart outlining fallout after previous spy scandals (M)

2001-03-24.title: Defection of Senior Chinese Officer Is Confirmed
LIVESTOCK EPIDEMIC WIDENS ITS MENACE FOR BRITISH FARMS
2001-03-24.abstract: Forecasts about Britain's foot-and-mouth epidemic worsen alarmingly, casting doubt on Prime Min Tony Blair's carefully nurtured plan to seek re-election in spring; number of affected farms and other sites is now expected to reach 4,000 by June; officials concede having lost control of spread of contagion because of delay between finding virus and slaughtering animals; Blair is reportedly dismayed and furious that true dimensions of crisis were kept from him until March 22; orders creation of two-square-mile 'firebreak cull' killing zones around every infected farm; says time between diagnosis and slaughter has been reduced to maximum of 24 hours; photos of cows being killed and heaped on pyre (M)

2001-03-24.title: Suspect Held in China Bombings
2001-03-24.abstract: Chinese police arrest Jin Ruchao and say he 'candidly confessed' to being responsible for explosions that killed more than 100 people in Shijiazhuang and an earlier murder (S)

2001-03-24.title: David McTaggart, a Builder of Greenpeace, Dies at 69
2001-03-24.abstract: David McTaggart, credited with building Greenpeace in worldwide environmental movement, dies at age 69; photo (M)

2001-03-24.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-03-24.abstract: THE AMERICAS    CANADA: RESIGNATION SOUGHT -- Prompted by the release of new information, opposition leaders accused Prime Minister Jean Chrétien of a conflict of interest and of concealing the truth, and called for him to step aside. The opposition has alleged for months that the prime minister pressured federal officials to approve a $390,000 loan for a failing hotel in which he held a 25 percent stake, though Mr. Chrétien has said that he sold his interest in 1993 before talking to officials. Toronto newspapers have now reported that Mr. Chrétien was listed on ownership records after he took office in November 1993.      Anthony DePalma (NYT)    GUYANA: RULING PARTY WINS -- Guyana's governing party has won a third consecutive term after racially charged elections that prompted accusations of fraud and touched off riots. According to official results, Monday's vote returned to power President Bharrat Jagdeo and  his People's Progressive Party, which is largely backed by Guyanese of East Indian descent.    (AP)

2001-03-25.title: The Bay of Pigs Revisited, but Arm in Arm
2001-03-25.abstract: Old enemies from Bay of Pigs invasion in Cuba hold reunion there; photo of former CIA official Robert Reynolds with Alfredo Duran, who was with invading force and went on to become Democratic leader in Florida; Duran, who was expelled from Brigade 2506 Veterans Assn for making trip, says most emotional moment was not meeting Fidel Castro, as he expected it to be, but meeting Cuban soldiers who fought them off (M)

2001-03-25.title: Diplomatically, French Is a Faded Rose in an English Garden
2001-03-25.abstract: English creeps up as diplomatic language at UN, replacing French; latest edition of UN's blue guide asks 189 UN member nations to list preferred language, and more than 120 choose English (M)

2001-03-25.title: Argentina Presses for Ways to Jolt Its Economy Out of Recession
2001-03-25.abstract: Argentina legislative leaders hold unusual weekend session to grant extraordinary powers to enable new economy minister, Domingo Cavallo, to cut taxes and labor costs to jump-start economy (M)

2001-03-25.title: Security Heightened as Bombs Kill 20 in 3 Russian Villages
2001-03-25.abstract: Three car bombs explode in Russian towns near Chechen border; photo (S)

2001-03-25.title: Macedonia Uses Helicopters Against Rebels for First Time
2001-03-25.abstract: Macedonia uses attack helicopters against ethnic Albanian rebels for first time, firing rockets at their positions above Tetovo (S)

2001-03-26.title: Britain Deploys the Army in Foot-and-Mouth Battle
2001-03-26.abstract: CARLISLE, England, March 26 - In a sharp escalation of its battle to contain an epidemic of foot and mouth disease, Britain deployed its army today to bury the first of up to half a million carcasses of both infected and healthy sheep in a mass grave the size of a football field on a disused airfield near this northwestern city.

2001-03-26.title: Mideast Fact-Finding Panel Angers Sharon
2001-03-26.abstract: Israel's Prime Min Ariel Sharon lashes out at American-sponsored fact-finding committee led by former Sen George J Mitchell, calling inquiry into conflict with Palestinians unjustified; says Israel made 'historic mistake' when it consented to investigation; committee was appointed by former Pres Bill Clinton, with support of Palestinians and previous Israeli government as part of failed cease-fire pact agreed to in October at Sharm el Sheik, Egypt; photos (M)

2001-03-26.title: Ellen Hammer, 79; Historian Wrote on French in Indochina
2001-03-26.abstract: Ellen J Hammer, historian who wrote about Indochina and Vietnam before Vietnam War, dies at age 79 (M)

2001-03-26.title: Kautokeino Journal; Reindeer Herders, at Home on a (Very Cold) Range
2001-03-26.abstract: Samis, reindeer herders in Kautokeino, Norway, whose traditon of animal husbandry dates back to Ice Age are locked in dispute with government conservationists seeking to regulate reindeer industry to protect wolves and other predators; say government's efforts have hurt business; map; photo (M)

2001-03-26.title: South Korean Leader Picks Old U.S. Hand as Foreign Minister
2001-03-26.abstract: South Korea's Pres Kim Dae Jung appoints Han Seung Soo, former ambassador to US during Republican administrations, in effort to improve strained relations with Washington; Han replaces Lee Juong Binn, who resigned after being blamed for series of blunders in dealing with administration on sensitive issue of relations with North Korea; served in Washington between 1986 and 1989; appointment is part of cabinet shakeup as Kim seeks to bolster his sagging popularity (M)

2001-03-27.title: 9 Aid Workers Held Hostage After Gunfight by Somalis
2001-03-27.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, March 27 — Six officials from the World Health Organization and Unicef, and three representatives of Doctors without Borders working on a child vaccination program in Somalia, were taken hostage today by gunmen in Mogadishu, the United Nations spokesman said here.

2001-03-27.title: Trying to Stem Foot-and-Mouth, Britain Buries Carcasses
2001-03-27.abstract: Britain deploys army to bury first of up to half million carcasses of infected and healthy sheep in mass grave in Cumbria County in sharp escalation of battle to contain epidemic of foot-and-mouth disease; map; photo (M)

2001-03-27.title: China Says U.S.-Based Scholar Is Spy for 'Overseas Agencies'
2001-03-27.abstract: BEIJING, March 27 — The Chinese government today accused a United States-based sociologist who has been held in isolation for more than six weeks of being a paid spy for "overseas intelligence agencies."

2001-03-27.title: Brazil Flexes New Muscle In Another Trade Fight
2001-03-27.abstract: Brazil flexes its economic muscle in trade dispute with US over patents on AIDS drugs, shedding image as easygoing and cordial; is rebutting US case before World Trade Organization which argues that Brazilian law, by forcing foreign companies to provide anti-AIDS drugs at lower prices or licensing Brazilian companies to do so in event of health emergency, violates international trade rules; says it will win not only by legal arguments and requirements of intellectual property law, but by moral dimensions of its case; runs highly successful program to fight AIDS, in part because of ability to provide drugs at prices below those charged by major pharmaceutical companies (M)

2001-03-27.title: 58 Youths Die in Kenyan Dormitory Fire
2001-03-27.abstract: Flames erupt in packed dormitory of secondary school in Machakos, Kenya, killing 58 sleeping schoolboys; authorities say blaze may have been deliberately set; launch probe; photo (M)

2001-03-28.title: Putin, in Big Shakeup, Fires Aides and Brings In Civilians
2001-03-28.abstract: MOSCOW, March 28 — President Vladimir V. Putin replaced his defense and interior ministers today and fired the atomic energy chief in the most extensive set of personnel changes in his year-old government. He also emphasized that he was putting civilians in key posts "as a step towards the demilitarization of society in Russia."

2001-03-28.title: Macedonia Attempts to Rout Rebels Before Talks
2001-03-28.abstract: LIPKOVO, Macedonia, Mar. 27 — Macedonian security forces launched another operation today against ethnic Albanian rebels, this time in northern Macedonia close to the border with Kosovo, in a last effort to rout the guerrillas before political talks begin.

2001-03-28.title: U.S. Vetoes Pro-Palestinian U.N. Resolution
2001-03-28.abstract: UNITED NATIONS, March 28 — The Bush administration cast its first veto in the Security Council late Tuesday night, ending a weeklong marathon debate and decisively scuttling Palestinian hopes for a United Nations observer force in the Israeli-occupied territories in the foreseeable future.

2001-03-28.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-03-28.abstract: EUROPE    UKRAINE: LEADING OPPONENT FREED -- A judge freed the former deputy prime minister, Yulia V. Tymoshenko, from jail. A leading opponent of President Leonid Kuchma, Ms. Tymoshenko was detained in February on corruption charges, which she asserts were politically motivated, after she led an effort to bring order to Ukraine's notoriously corrupt energy sector. Judge Mykola Zamkovenko overturned an arrest warrant issued by the general prosecutor, though criminal proceedings are still pending.      Patrick E. Tyler (NYT)    GERMANY: NUCLEAR SHIPMENT -- The German police said they had detained more than 400 people in protests over a shipment of nuclear waste from France to an underground storage site in central Germany. Anti-nuclear activists tried to halt an armored train carrying six containers of waste to the bunker in Gorleben. The police removed about 1,000 people who staged a sit-down on a main railroad near Lüneburg.      Victor Homola (NYT)

2001-03-28.title: Beijing Publishes Detailed Account of Bombings
2001-03-28.abstract: Chinese government, in attempt to quell fears and rumors about series of explosions in Shijiazhuang two weeks ago that killed more than 100 people, publishes detailed account of how it says one disgruntled man carried out bombings; Jin Ruchao was arrested last week, but until now police had released little information about investigation, prompting widespread skepticism over whether they had caught real culprit (M)

2001-03-29.title: Before Talks, Macedonia Tries to Keep Albanian Rebels in Check
2001-03-29.abstract: Macedonian security forces mount another operation against ethnic Albanian rebels in last effort to rout guerrillas before political talks begin; government says it has successfully driven back rebels from area around Tetovo and now has only few villages in north to deal with; latest bombardment angers Albanians; Albanian political parties are making same demands as rebels, namely revisions in constitution and equal rights for Albanians in education, employment and use of their own language; photo (M)

2001-03-29.title: Fighting Spills Into Kosovo, Killing 3 and Wounding 16
2001-03-29.abstract: SKOPJE, Macedonia, March 29 — Fighting between Macedonian security forces and ethnic Albanian rebels in the north spilled over the border into Kosovo today, killing three people and wounding 16.

2001-03-29.title: U.S. REVIEWING AID MEANT TO CONTAIN RUSSIA'S ARSENAL
2001-03-29.abstract: Bush administration begins comprehensive review of all American aid programs to Russia set up to stop spread of nuclear, biological and chemical weapons; Washington now spends more than $760 million a year trying to dismantle former Soviet nuclear, biological and chemical complexes and prevent unconventional weapons and hazardous materials from being sold to rogue states and terrorist groups or stolen by them; senior official says several programs seem 'very effective,' but others are not; cites $6 billion longterm effort to help dispose of 34 metric tons of weapons-grade plutonium as money not well spent; review comes at time of growing tension between Russia and United States; Russian officials accuse Washington of being intent on reviving cold war (M)

2001-03-29.title: A New Theory In Egyptair Crash
2001-03-29.abstract: American investigators studying crash of Egyptair jet in 1999 say they are looking into control problem with same model plane that occurred Mar 27 as it approached Paris; it involved American Airlines Boeing 767, which landed with no injuries (S)

2001-03-29.title: Layoffs Cast Doubt on Burst of U.S. Confidence
2001-03-29.abstract: NEW YORK—  As the parade of layoff announcements from industrial companies around the world grew on Wednesday, economists and investors questioned the optimism of American consumers that brightened the stock market just 24 hours earlier.

2001-03-30.title: WORLD BRIEFING
2001-03-30.abstract: UNITED NATIONS    MEASLES KILLS 900,000 -- More than 30 million children still get measles every year and 900,000 die, accounting for more than half the 1.6 million childhood deaths that could be prevented with vaccinations, said the World Health Organization and Unicef. The agencies hope to halve the deaths by 2005 by giving all infants at least one dose of vaccine. Immunizing a child costs 26 cents.       Barbara Crossette (NYT)

2001-03-30.title: Bush Hammers Arafat; Takes a Softer Tone With Israel
2001-03-30.abstract: Pres Bush, speaking at second news conference of his presidency, makes most expansive statements on Middle East since assuming office; says Palestinian leader, Yasir Arafat, should hear 'loud and clear' that United States wants him to 'stop the violence'; Bush urges Israel to exercise restraint in its military actions but reserves most pointed language for Arafat; Bush says he has instructed Secretary of State Colin L Powell to call Arafat to reiterate his message on halting violence; administration officials say strong words about Arafat and renewed frustration with him stem in part from his demands for vote on Security Council resolution seeking United Nations observer force in Israeli occupied territories; United States vetoed move; photo (M)

2001-03-30.title: Arms Control Nominee Defends Shifting View
2001-03-30.abstract: John R Bolton, nominee for under secretary of state for arms control and international security at State Dept, tries to explain at his confirmation hearing his seeming about-face on test-ban treaty; he previously characterized supporters of treaty as 'misguided'; now he says he would examine elements of treaty as part of overall strategic review (S)

2001-03-30.title: 10,000 Israeli Arabs Protest in Sympathy With Palestinians
2001-03-30.abstract: SAKHNIN, Israel, March 30 — In the Israeli-Arab community, life is divided into before and after last October, when Israeli police shot dead 13 Israeli Arabs during tense, raucous demonstrations. For many, hope died then that Israeli Arabs would ever be accepted as full citizens of Israel with equal status and rights.

2001-03-30.title: Helge Ingstad, Discoverer of Viking Site, Is Dead at 101
2001-03-30.abstract: Helge Ingstad, Norwegian writer and adventurer who followed hunch and ancient map to identify place where Vikings landed in North America 500 years before Columbus, dies at age 101; photo (M)

2001-03-31.title: To Men, Losing to Judit Polgar Is No Disgrace : Tigress of the Chessboard Purrs Quietly and Pounces
2001-03-31.abstract: CANNES—  Judit Polgar, the strongest woman chess player ever, was one move away from checkmating Christian Bauer, a French grandmaster. He resigned.

2001-03-31.title: European Union Takes Step Toward Ties With Pyongyang
2001-03-31.abstract: european union takes step toward ties with pyongyangÿ

2001-03-31.title: 30 Villagers Presumed Dead After Paramilitary Raid in Colombia
2001-03-31.abstract: Narino Province, Colombia, Gov Parmenio Cuellar reports some 200 to 300 right-wing paramilitary group raiders marched into Llorente, herded residents into plaza and seized 30 people it accused of collaborating with leftist guerrillas; says 30 are now presumed dead; says security forced did not arrive until five days later; he and human rights officials see raid linked to extensive American-backed fumigation of coca fields in neighboring Putumayo Province, which has displaced coca growers, rebels and paramilitary gunmen to once quiet Llorente region (M)

2001-03-31.title: Portage la Prairie Journal; Beavers' Dual Role: National Icon and Huge Pest
2001-03-31.abstract: Beaver, North America's largest rodent and Canada's national symbol, has also become major pest; population has exploded in Manitoba as antifur movement has depressed demand and prices for pelts; animals' dam-building floods farms and golf courses, blocks streets and railroads and drops trees on power lines and cottages; province now pays bounty for beaver tails; map; photo (M)

2001-03-31.title: China Holds American
2001-03-31.abstract: Chinese security agents detained a Chinese-born American last month as he crossed the border from Hong Kong, where he works as a business professor, the American Embassy confirmed. The unexplained detention of Li Shaomin, a naturalized citizen, follows several other incidents in which Chinese-born scholars with United States ties have been held on vague security grounds.      Article, Page A7.
